figures.caption: Table 2: Estimation of p(b | Y ) = P0(maxA |ZA| > b | Y ) conditional on the expression values of four genes in a single-cell eQTL study of the OneK1K cohort. Reported are the p-value estimates from IS (p̂IS), ASSET analytic approximation (p̂ASSET), and näıve MC (p̂MC), together with E = KMC/KIS, the nominal efficiency gain of IS over näıve MC. Results are shown for random subsamples (N = 100, 200) and the full cohort (N = 981). IS uses 50,000 simulations; MC uses KMC simulations calibrated to achieve se(p̂)/p = 0.1.

Figure 1: Simulation results for with M = 7 independent studies. Left: Curves of − log10{p(b)} versus b obtained using IS, ASSET analytic approximation, and naive Monte Carlo (MC). Right: Nominal efficiency E = KMC/KIS of IS over naive MC. Here, KIS and KMC are the numbers of simulations required to achieve a standard error of 0.1× p(b).

Figure 3: Scatter plot of − log10{p(b)} comparing the conditional IS p-values and the theoretical ASSET (DLM) p-values for the observed ASSET statistic ZASSET = maxA |ZA|. Points are faceted by minor allele frequency (MAF) categories: MAF < 10%, 10% ≤ MAF ≤ 20%, and MAF > 20%. The red dashed line denotes the 45◦ line of equality.

Figure 2: Curves of − log10{p(b | Y )} versus b for (a) TPT1 and (b) CDKN1A from the oneK1K data. Rows correspond to subsample sizes (N = 100, 200) and the full dataset (N = 981), and columns correspond to allele frequencies MAF ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50}. Estimates from importance sampling (IS), ASSET, and näıve Monte Carlo (MC) are shown. IS uses a single tilting parameter within each neighborhood of b values.

Table 1: Estimation of p(b) = P0(maxA |ZA| > b). The quantities p̂MVN, p̂ASSET, p̂IS, and p̂MC denote p-value estimates from the R function pmvnorm applied to the (2M − 1)-dimensional joint distribution of the {ZA}, the ASSET analytic approximation, importance sampling, and näıve Monte Carlo, respectively; standard errors for IS and MC are shown in parentheses. KIS and KMC are the numbers of simulations required to achieve se(p̂)/p = 0.1, and E = KMC/KIS denotes the nominal efficiency gain. IS estimates use 5× 104 iterations; MC is run using KMC simulations where feasible, and entries are omitted (“–”) where p is too small for MC to be practical.
